questions.prompt: A researcher wants to measure infants’ ability to discriminate between two similar speech sounds by tracking changes in looking time across repeated trials. Which method is MOST appropriate?
Which statement BEST describes synaptic pruning?
options: A process where unused neural connections are eliminated, shaped by experience
A process where axons are coated to speed neural signals
A process where new neurons are produced throughout infancy at a constant rate
A process where hemispheres become specialized for different functions

questions.prompt: Myelination is important in development primarily because it:
options: Creates new synapses between all neurons
Increases the speed and efficiency of neural communication
Eliminates neurons that are not used

questions.prompt: Which developmental trend describes growth from the center of the body outward toward the fingers and toes?
A baby reaches for a toy, misses repeatedly, then gradually begins to coordinate the arm and hand movements to grasp it. This illustrates that reaching development is best understood as:
options: A fixed maturational sequence independent of experience
An outcome of interacting systems that self-organize over time
A reflex that simply persists into later life
A skill that transfers automatically from crawling to walking

questions.prompt: A key claim of dynamic systems theory is that motor skills:
options: Emerge from interactions among multiple components (body, environment, goals)
Are genetically programmed and appear once the brain reaches a set age
Transfer directly from one skill to another with no reorganization
Depend mostly on reflexes and their disappearance

questions.prompt: Which best describes intermodal perception?
options: The ability to perceive depth using binocular cues
The ability to integrate information across different senses (e.g., sight and sound)
The ability to recognize oneself in a mirror
The ability to imitate facial gestures

questions.prompt: An infant is shown the same image repeatedly until looking time decreases, then shown a new image and looking time increases. The increase suggests:
Perceptual narrowing in the first year is MOST consistent with which outcome?
options: Improved discrimination for both familiar and unfamiliar faces/phonemes equally
Reduced specialization for stimuli in the infant’s environment
Increased specialization for familiar faces/phonemes and reduced discrimination of unfamiliar ones
A general loss of visual acuity after 6 months

questions.prompt: Why can early cataracts have long-term effects even if corrected later?
options: They reduce early patterned visual input during a sensitive period for perception
They cause the child to develop avoidant attachment
They increase perceptual narrowing for all stimuli

questions.prompt: In Piaget’s theory, a scheme is BEST defined as:
options: An organized pattern of action/thought used to interpret experiences
A reflex that disappears with maturation

questions.prompt: A toddler sees a new animal (a zebra) and calls it “horse.” This is an example of:
A child modifies their concept of “horse” after learning zebras are different. This is an example of:
A major criticism of Piaget’s object permanence conclusions is that his tasks:
options: May underestimate infants because they require coordinated motor responses
Only apply to children older than 5
Overestimate infants because they are too easy

questions.prompt: Baillargeon’s drawbridge (violation-of-expectation) findings are often interpreted to suggest that infants:
options: Have no understanding of object continuity until they can search manually
May show earlier understanding than Piaget’s manual search tasks indicate
Learn object permanence only through reinforcement
Cannot represent hidden objects at any age

questions.prompt: Which language subsystem is MOST directly concerned with sentence structure?
A morpheme is:
A child says “want cookie.” This is MOST characteristic of:
A parent responds to “Doggy run” with “Yes, the dog is running fast!” This is BEST described as:
Which statement BEST captures the ‘problem of reference’ in word learning?
options: A new word could map onto many possible referents in the environment
Children learn verbs before nouns universally
Children must be explicitly taught grammar rules

questions.prompt: In temperament research, Chess & Thomas are MOST known for proposing:
options: Inhibited vs uninhibited categories based on novelty
Reactivity plus self-regulation with 3 broad dimensions
Nine dimensions and three temperament types (easy/difficult/slow-to-warm-up)

questions.prompt: Kagan’s temperament theory emphasizes individual differences in:
options: Behavioral inhibition in response to novelty
Parent sensitivity as the cause of temperament
Syntax acquisition in the first year

questions.prompt: Rothbart & Bates’ concept of effortful control MOST closely refers to:
The goodness-of-fit model predicts the best outcomes when:
options: Parents use the same approach for all children
Parenting is matched to the child’s temperament and supports regulation
Temperament is ignored to promote independence
Children adapt only by changing their temperament

questions.prompt: A 12-month-old becomes distressed when the caregiver leaves, but is quickly soothed and returns to play when the caregiver returns. This pattern in the Strange Situation is MOST consistent with:
